1	import { test } from '@playwright/test'
2	
3	// DDx Server UI — Demo Recording (TP-002 TC-009)
4	//
5	// Produces a polished video walkthrough of all 6 pages with real data
6	// interactions. Designed for embedding in the DDx microsite.
7	//
8	// Run:
9	//   bun run demo:record
10	// Output:
11	//   demo-output/ contains a .webm video file
12	
13	async function getFixtureIds(
14		request: import('@playwright/test').APIRequestContext
15	): Promise<{ nodeId: string; projectId: string }> {
16		const nodeResp = await request.post('/graphql', {
17			data: { query: '{ nodeInfo { id name } }' }
18		})
19		const nodeBody = (await nodeResp.json()) as {
20			data: { nodeInfo: { id: string; name: string } }
21		}
22		const projectsResp = await request.get('/api/projects')
23		const projects = (await projectsResp.json()) as Array<{
24			id: string
25			name: string
26			path: string
27		}>
28		const fixture = projects.find(
29			(p) => /(^|\/)ddx-e2e-/.test(p.path) || /^ddx-e2e-/.test(p.name)
30		)
31		if (!fixture) {
32			throw new Error(
33				`fixture server has no ddx-e2e-* project registered (got: ${projects
34					.map((p) => p.id)
35					.join(', ')})`
36			)
37		}
38		return { nodeId: nodeBody.data.nodeInfo.id, projectId: fixture.id }
39	}
40	
41	test.describe('DDx Server UI Demo', () => {
42	  test('full walkthrough', async ({ page, request }) => {
43	    const ids = await getFixtureIds(request)
44	    const base = `/nodes/${ids.nodeId}/projects/${ids.projectId}`
45	
46	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
47	    // 1. Project Overview — overview of the project
48	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
49	    await test.step('Project Overview — overview of the project', async () => {
50	      await page.goto(base)
51	      await page.waitForSelector('h1')
52	      await page.waitForLoadState('networkidle')
53	      await page.waitForTimeout(2500)
54	    })
55	
56	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
57	    // 2. Documents — browse and read a document
58	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
59	    await test.step('Documents — browse and read a document', async () => {
60	      await page.goto(`${base}/documents`)
61	      await page.waitForSelector('h1')
62	      await page.waitForTimeout(1000)
63	
64	      // Select the first document to show rendered markdown
65	      const firstDoc = page.locator('.overflow-auto button').first()
66	      if (await firstDoc.isVisible({ timeout: 3000 })) {
67	        await firstDoc.click()
68	        await page.waitForSelector('.prose', { timeout: 5000 })
69	        await page.waitForTimeout(2000)
70	      }
71	
72	      // Demonstrate search
73	      const docSearch = page.locator('input[placeholder*="Search"]')
74	      if (await docSearch.isVisible()) {
75	        await docSearch.fill('persona')
76	        await page.waitForTimeout(1000)
77	        await docSearch.fill('')
78	        await page.waitForTimeout(500)
79	      }
80	    })
81	
82	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
83	    // 3. Beads — kanban board, search, detail, create
84	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
85	    await test.step('Beads — kanban board, search, detail, create', async () => {
86	      await page.goto(`${base}/beads`)
87	      await page.waitForSelector('text=OPEN')
88	      await page.waitForTimeout(1500)
89	
90	      // Search for beads (type="search" distinguishes it from the command palette input)
91	      const beadSearch = page.locator('input[type="search"]')
92	      if (await beadSearch.isVisible()) {
93	        await beadSearch.fill('open')
94	        await page.waitForTimeout(1200)
95	        await beadSearch.fill('')
96	        await page.waitForTimeout(600)
97	      }
98	
99	      // Click a bead card to show detail panel
100	      const beadCard = page.locator('[draggable="true"]').first()
101	      if (await beadCard.isVisible({ timeout: 3000 }).catch(() => false)) {
102	        await beadCard.click()
103	        await page.waitForTimeout(2000)
104	      }
105	    })
106	
107	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
108	    // 4. Graph — document dependency visualization
109	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
110	    await test.step('Graph — document dependency visualization', async () => {
111	      await page.goto(`${base}/graph`)
112	      await page.waitForSelector('h1')
113	      await page.waitForTimeout(2500)
114	    })
115	
116	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
117	    // 5. Workers — session history
118	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
119	    await test.step('Workers — session history', async () => {
120	      await page.goto(`${base}/workers`)
121	      await page.waitForSelector('h1')
122	      await page.waitForTimeout(2000)
123	    })
124	
125	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
126	    // 6. Personas — browse and view a persona
127	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
128	    await test.step('Personas — browse and view a persona', async () => {
129	      await page.goto(`${base}/personas`)
130	      await page.waitForSelector('text=Personas')
131	      await page.waitForTimeout(2000)
132	
133	      const firstPersona = page.locator('.w-80 button').first()
134	      if (await firstPersona.isVisible({ timeout: 2000 }).catch(() => false)) {
135	        await firstPersona.click()
136	        await page.waitForTimeout(2000)
137	      }
138	    })
139	
140	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
141	    // 7. Back to Project Overview — closing shot
142	    // ---------------------------------------------------------------
143	    await test.step('Back to Project Overview — closing shot', async () => {
144	      await page.goto(base)
145	      await page.waitForSelector('h1')
146	      await page.waitForTimeout(2000)
147	    })
148	  })
149	})
150

_index.md
---
---
title: Features
description: "DDx platform capabilities — stable, beta, and planned."
---

DDx is a focused set of platform primitives for AI-assisted development. Each capability below carries a maturity badge so you can tell what is solid today from what is still in motion.

## Artifact Graph {#artifact-graph} {{< maturity "beta" >}}

![Artifact graph view](/ui/feature-artifact-graph.png "Artifact graph — placeholder")

Documents are not the only thing a project ships. Diagrams, screenshots, schema files, fixtures, generated assets — all of them carry meaning, and all of them drift. DDx treats every artifact as a first-class node in a single graph: markdown documents directly, and non-markdown files (graphics, diagrams, binaries) through a `.ddx.yaml` sidecar that records kind, provenance, and links.

The graph exists to answer two questions:

- **Drift visibility** — which artifacts have moved out from under the documents that reference them, and which references now point at stale or missing targets?
- **Impact analysis** — if you change this spec, which features, beads, diagrams, and downstream artifacts are implicated?

```
ddx doc graph         # show the artifact graph
ddx doc audit         # surface drift and broken references
```

This is the spine the rest of the platform hangs from. Beads reference artifacts; executions produce artifacts; reviews assess artifacts. Keeping the graph honest keeps everything else honest.

## Beads & DAG {#beads-dag} {{< maturity "stable" >}}

![Bead list with dependency tree](/ui/feature-beads-dag.png "Beads and dependency DAG — placeholder")

Beads are the atomic unit of work. Each one carries a title, description, acceptance criteria, labels, state history, and a typed dependency edge to other beads. The dependency graph is a DAG, not a list: `ddx bead ready` returns only beads whose prerequisites are closed, so the queue is always a correct-by-construction work front.

```
ddx bead create "Add login endpoint" --ac "returns 200 on valid credentials"
ddx bead dep add <child> --on <parent>
ddx bead ready
ddx bead tree <id>
```

## Execute-Loop (`ddx work`) {#execute-loop} {{< maturity "stable" >}}

![Execute-loop draining the queue](/ui/feature-execute-loop.png "ddx work draining the queue — placeholder")

`ddx work` drains the bead queue. It picks the next ready bead, runs the configured agent harness inside an isolated git worktree, captures evidence, and merges the result back when acceptance criteria pass. Failing or timed-out runs are preserved as refs for inspection rather than discarded.

```
ddx work                              # drain the queue
ddx agent execute-bead <id> --from HEAD
```

Because each iteration runs in its own worktree, parallel runs are safe and a failed run never poisons the main branch.

### Why a loop, not a long session — bounded context execution

The execute-loop is the operational shape of **bounded context execution** — sometimes called the **Ralph loop**. LLM output quality decays as a single context window fills: transcript, tool output, and failed attempts accumulate and compete with the original instructions. We call this decay **context rot**, and it is why long-running agent sessions are unreliable even on frontier models.

`ddx work` answers context rot structurally. Each bead runs in a fresh agent invocation with a clean context, scoped tightly to the bead's description, acceptance criteria, and the files it touches. When the attempt ends — merged, preserved, or abandoned — the context is discarded. Persistent state lands on disk as evidence, code, an updated bead, or a `<review-findings>` block threaded into the next attempt's prompt. The next iteration starts cold and reads what it needs from the substrate.

The result: drain throughput stays steady across hundreds of beads. The agent never has to remember what attempt 47 was thinking. The loop does the remembering, on disk, in plain files.

See [Bounded Context Execution](/docs/concepts/bounded-context-execution/) for the full treatment.

1	// Quorum review (multi-model review) end-to-end UI flow.
2	//
3	// Driving spec for the quorum-review UI surfaced from
4	// `ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=a,b,c`. The flow under test:
5	//
6	//   1. Operator opens a bead (or artifact) and dispatches a quorum review,
7	//      picking ≥2 harnesses and a verdict policy (majority / unanimous).
8	//   2. Server fans out to each harness and streams per-model findings.
9	//   3. UI shows a per-arm column with verdict + structured findings, plus a
10	//      rollup verdict computed from the policy.
11	//   4. Operator can drill into a single arm's raw output and link back to the
12	//      originating bead / artifact.
13	//
14	// The quorum-review view is not yet implemented (PRD §437 "Multi-agent review
15	// workflow produces structured findings from quorum"). These tests are checked
16	// in as a TDD seed: every case is marked `test.fixme` so CI stays green; once
17	// the view ships, drop the fixme markers one case at a time as features land.
18	// Mocks below describe the GraphQL contract the view is expected to consume.
19	
20	import { expect, test, type Page } from '@playwright/test';
21	
22	const NODE_INFO = { id: 'node-abc', name: 'Test Node' };
23	const PROJECT_ID = 'proj-1';
24	const PROJECTS = [{ id: PROJECT_ID, name: 'Project Alpha', path: '/repos/alpha' }];
25	const BEAD_ID = 'ddx-quorum-001';
26	
27	const REVIEW_BASE_URL = `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads/${BEAD_ID}/reviews/quorum`;
28	
29	const HARNESSES = [
30		{ name: 'claude', provider: 'anthropic', model: 'claude-opus-4-7' },
31		{ name: 'codex', provider: 'openai', model: 'gpt-5.4' },
32		{ name: 'gemini', provider: 'google', model: 'gemini-2.5-pro' }
33	];
34	
35	const REVIEW_ID = 'qrv-001';
36	
37	const QUORUM_REVIEW = {
38		id: REVIEW_ID,
39		beadId: BEAD_ID,
40		policy: 'majority',

62	## Evidence Capture {#evidence-capture} {{< maturity "stable" >}}
63	
64	![Execution evidence bundle](/ui/feature-evidence-capture.png "Evidence capture bundle — placeholder")
65	
66	Every execution writes a bundle under `.ddx/executions/<timestamp>-<hash>/` with the prompt, the agent transcript, token and cost telemetry, the diff produced, and the merge outcome. Evidence stays in the repository, attached to the commit, so future reviewers (human or model) can reconstruct what happened without rerunning the agent.
67	
68	```
69	ddx agent log
70	ls .ddx/executions/
71	```
72	
73	## Multi-Model Review {#multi-model-review} {{< maturity "beta" >}}
74	
75	![Multi-model review consensus](/ui/feature-multi-model-review.png "Multi-model review — placeholder")
76	
77	A single model is a single point of view. DDx can dispatch the same prompt — a plan, a diff, a spec — to multiple harnesses (Claude, Codex, Gemini, local models) and aggregate their findings. Quorum modes let you require majority or unanimous agreement before a result is treated as approved.
78	
79	```
80	ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini --prompt review.md
81	```
82	
83	The pattern works for plan review before implementation, diff review before merge, and spec sanity-checks before breakdown into beads.
84	
85	## Skills {#skills} {{< maturity "beta" >}}
86	
87	![Skill listing](/ui/feature-skills.png "Skills — placeholder")
88	
89	Skills are reusable, agent-invocable capabilities packaged alongside the project. They install under `.agents/skills/` and `.claude/skills/` and become available to any harness that supports skill discovery. Plugins ship skills the way they ship templates and prompts — versioned, project-local, no global state.
90	
91	```
92	ddx install <plugin-name>     # plugins can carry skills
93	ls .claude/skills/
94	```
95	
96	## Agent-Agnostic Dispatch {#agent-agnostic-dispatch} {{< maturity "stable" >}}
97	
98	![Agent harness selection](/ui/feature-agent-dispatch.png "Agent-agnostic dispatch — placeholder")
99	
100	DDx talks to agents through a uniform harness interface. Swap Claude for Codex for a local model by changing configuration, not commands. The same `ddx agent run` invocation works across providers; cost, tokens, and latency are recorded the same way regardless of which harness answered.
101	
102	```
103	ddx agent list                # show available harnesses
104	ddx agent run --harness claude --prompt prompts/implement.md
105	ddx agent run --harness codex --prompt prompts/implement.md
106	ddx agent doctor              # harness health check
107	```
108	
109	This is what makes cost-tiered routing and multi-model review possible: the rest of the platform never has to care which model is on the other end of the wire.
110

test(e2e): extend screenshots + demo-recording for 7 feature areas [ddx-98e49546]

screenshots.spec.ts: add a marketing-screenshot describe block that captures
one full-page PNG per feature in website/content/features/_index.md and writes
it directly into website/static/ui/ under the filename the Hugo site renders
(feature-artifact-graph.png, feature-beads-dag.png, feature-execute-loop.png,
feature-evidence-capture.png, feature-multi-model-review.png, feature-skills.png,
feature-agent-dispatch.png).

demo-recording.spec.ts: add a per-feature describe with video forced on; each
clip walks one feature area and copies the recorded webm to website/static/ui/
under the matching feature-*.webm filename.

Routes pulled from src/routes — graph, beads (with detail), workers,
executions, runs, plugins, sessions. Some target views are still gap-bead
work (multi-model review, etc.); the bead AC explicitly notes the spec
passes after those land.
EOF
)"
